title: Ensure that the admission control policy is set to ServiceAccount

input_row.title: Ensure that the admission control policy is set to ServiceAccount
input_row.rationale: When you create a pod, if you do not specify a service account, it is automatically assigned the default service account in the same namespace. You should create your own service account and let the API server manage its security tokens.
input_row.audit: Run the following command on the master node: ps -ef | grep kube-apiserver Verify that the --admission-control argument is set to a value that includes ServiceAccount.
input_row.remediation: Follow the documentation and create ServiceAccount objects as per your environment. Then, edit the /etc/kubernetes/apiserver file on the master node and set the KUBE_ADMISSION_CONTROL parameter to "--admission- control=...,ServiceAccount,...": KUBE_ADMISSION_CONTROL="--admission-control=...,ServiceAccount,..." Based on your system, restart the kube-apiserver service. For example: systemctl restart kube-apiserver.service Impact: The ServiceAccount objects must be created and granted before pod creation would be allowed. Default Value: By default, ServiceAccount is not set.  67 | P a g e References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/admin/kube-apiserver/ 2. https://kubernetes.io/docs/admin/admission-controllers/#serviceaccount 3. https://kubernetes.io/docs/tasks/configure-pod-container/configure-service- account/
input_row.impact: The ServiceAccount objects must be created and granted before pod creation would be allowed. Default Value: By default, ServiceAccount is not set.  67 | P a g e

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Automated

**PROGRAM NAME:** k8s_api_server_admission_plugin_serviceaccount_enabled

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
- Query the Kubernetes API server configuration to verify that the "ServiceAccount" admission plugin is enabled.
- This can be checked by examining the arguments passed to the kube-apiserver process, specifically the `--enable-admission-plugins` flag (for Kubernetes 1.10+) or the deprecated `--admission-control` flag (for older versions).
- Use the following methods:
  - If running in a managed environment (e.g., kubeadm, static pods), retrieve the kube-apiserver manifest (typically at `/etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-apiserver.yaml`) and parse the command line arguments.
  - Alternatively, use `kubectl -n kube-system get pod -l component=kube-apiserver -o yaml` to retrieve the running pod spec and inspect the `command` or `args` fields.
- Pass Criteria:
  - The `--enable-admission-plugins` flag (or `--admission-control` for legacy clusters) must include `ServiceAccount` in its comma-separated list of plugins.
- Example CLI commands:
  - `kubectl -n kube-system get pod -l component=kube-apiserver -o jsonpath="{.items[*].spec.containers[*].command}"`
  - Or, for static pod clusters, inspect `/etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-apiserver.yaml` for the presence of `--enable-admission-plugins=...,ServiceAccount,...` or `--admission-control=...,ServiceAccount,...`
- Fail Criteria:
  - If the `ServiceAccount` plugin is not present in the enabled admission plugins list, the control fails.
- This check is fully automatable as it relies on the configuration of the kube-apiserver, which is accessible via the Kubernetes API or by inspecting the static pod manifest. No subjective review or host OS access is required beyond reading the pod spec or manifest.
